speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE] interview with Mr. Henry Blasenstein. So Mr. Blasenstein, you were describing your arrival at Plaszow of that you saw these three-- three people being hanged. And then you were sent to barracks.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- what else happened when you first arrived at Plaszow?

speaker: interviewee
text: What happened, a lot of things because I-- when-- when we arrived Plaszow, there was an old Jewish cemetery, a cemetery from the 1700s, you know, from early 1800s. We read the stones. And we-- we were breaking up the stones. We were making a sidewalk right in front of the commander-- commandant of the-- of the Plaszow concentration camp. His name was [NON-ENGLISH]. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And that took some time. Because there was a-- a big cemetery, you know. And there were a lot of people in that-- in that camp. But they were sent out for after the liquidation of the Krakow ghetto. Then we started to build more barracks, because they used to send in more and more people used to come in there. And then, I was in a group of people. We were sent in to Krakow, to where used to be the Krakow ghetto. And we used to empty the apartment from the people who were deported.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, when you say empty the apartment, what-- what---

speaker: interviewee
text: The apartment were like, like, let's say you get up in the morning, you know. The bed is not made. Everything was thrown on the floor because the people wanted to take it with them, you know. I imagine the people were just-- items what they could use, you know, and the rest, like, you know when you empty out drawers or something in a hurry. So you're just pulling to throw what you don't want to take. You throw on the floor, you know. And that's how it looked the apartments when we got in there. And we supposed to empty out everything. And trucks, load on the trucks, and they were sent in to Plaszow everything, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you ever able to find anything either valuable or helpful to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. You-- No, but I remember-- never forget-- forget one thing being in Krakow ghetto. We were taking out [SIGHS] there was-- hm, how do I explain that? You know what a [NON-ENGLISH] is?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know what a [NON-ENGLISH] is?

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: So there was-- you know what they heat the water in it. There is a big kettle, you know, for the heat the-- heat the water for the-- for the-- for the bath.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we were-- we supposed to take that out. And we [INAUDIBLE] didn't work that time. And in came in an SS man. He was one hand only, one arm. His name was [? Richie. ?] And he caught us all, you know, like, standing, not working. That was the first time that I got 25 on my behind, you know. Everyone got 25 on behind that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: 25 lashes?

speaker: interviewee
text: Lashes on the behind, yeah. And he did it by himself with one arm, you know. Never forget that, you know. Your behind was sore was sore for weeks after, you know. And then I used to be sent to other places. You know, I was in-- in a group for they used to do all kind of jobs, you know. Whenever you needed any jobs, they used to send us. And there is a one thing what I never forget either. You know, they used to catch a lot Jews-- Aryan. And as-- the day they caught them, they brought them to Plaszow. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Because one day, we were working. I don't remember, you know, when. And one SS man calls in me and another two people. And he tells us to take the wheelbarrow and to follow them. And that time came maybe 10 people. And we were going, like, up the hill. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And they told the people to undress, and they shot them. And we had to take the clothes back in on the wheelbarrows. And there were men and women and children, you know. And that happened very often there, you know. But that was one time when I was-- when I was not called, but I was ordered to-- to-- to go to do that. And there was a lot shooting there. It was a very dangerous place. You see, for example, if you had to go to the bathroom-- they used to call a [GERMAN]. They used to call it in German, a bathroom. And that could be a few blocks away from-- from-- from the block where we reduced to-- where you-- you used to live. And it came in the night-- night. At night, you might have to go a few times, you know, because you were cold. And so many times, you were afraid even to go, you know, because they may shot you during the night as you walk to the-- to the bathroom. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And then they enlarged again the-- the-- the Plaszow. They added more barracks. I never forget they-- they brought a whole train with food to build the barracks. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And the train was maybe a mile away from the-- from the camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they took the whole camp, a few thousand people. And you have to walk to the train and bring that-- bring that food and the building material into the camp. And I never forget. I was walking with-- we were carrying like long pieces of wood, you know. And I was in the back, and another man was in the front, like he was the leader.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then passed by a-- a-- an SS man. And he claimed he didn't walk too fast, and he shot him. And he killed him. So he took a-- a man who was going back. He took him, and he told him, pick it up the front and-- and-- and bring that food to this-- this place where they-- you know. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Then I'm sure you know, remember, from the movie, They were building a-- in Plaszow, they were building a living quarters for the Ukrainian, for the Ukrainian guard. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And-- and the builder's were like me, and you know, hundreds of other ones. You know, what they-- nobody knew about building a barracks, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when the skeleton was-- was-- was made finished already, everything collapsed like a box of matches. And there was a woman-- they don't show that in the-- in the-- in the-- in the movie. But there was a woman. She had a hitch back. I would call her a hutch back, you know. A hunchback?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, she was the architect from that building, that barrack. She was maybe 5, 10 feet away from me. And they shot her on the spot, because it was her fault that that barrack collapsed. So then we started to build again that-- that barrack. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And now, when I get to how I got to Schindler, or you want to ask other questions?

speaker: interviewer
text: Before we get to Schindler, when you were at Plaszow, you mentioned when you first got there having to break down the tombstones in the cemetery.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did that feel like? What?

speaker: interviewee
text: Terrible. And you know, you had to put the-- the stones with the writing up, not down. You know, when you built outside of it so that the SS men can work on the-- on the writing of the-- of the stones. And there were dates there, 1820, you know, from 1850, you know, long, long time ago.

speaker: interviewer
text: When in Plaszow, was there any particular uniform that you were given to wear?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we were wearing-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't know how you-- how you say in-- in English. There was-- in Polish, they used to call [POLISH]. That was-- it was the uniform [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] made with whites, with black stripes. I have a picture of-- of [INAUDIBLE]. Yes, and we were wearing-- we used to have numbers. Here, we used to have numbers.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever receive a number on your arm?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I received ones on my left arm KL-- KZ, but it mean 'concentration camp.' But it disappeared after awhile. And that I got by Schindler, by the way, not in Plaszow.

speaker: interviewer
text: You had mentioned at Klaj always being hungry. Did that change at all in Plaszow?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was even worse. You see, after-- after work, I used to always run close by the kitchen to figure maybe somebody's going to throw out something. But I wasn't the only-- only one. I-- there was hundreds of people who were trying. And I was, you know, a young boy.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they always push you in the back. I was in the back. I didn't have no chance at all to get nothing because there were people who were older, you know, stronger, you know. So if they did have something left, they used to give out, you know. But some soup or piece of bread, but I didn't have no chance to get that. But I still went there every single night. How long were you at Plaszow all altogether, roughly?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was in Plaszow for intervals, you know. In other words, I was in Plaszow when I went to Schindler. And down from Schindler from Krakow, I went back to-- to Plaszow and [INAUDIBLE] back again to Schindler. In other words, I was not straight in one time there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. When-- when you were at Plaszow, for example, were you ever there for either Pesach or Yom Kippur or any other?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah, I think I was there during all the holidays.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there--

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember the religious Jews, you know, they used to pray at night when-- after they came from work. Well, they prayed standing. But they-- and they had the same soup. There was nothing good what I-- what I could do, you know. And yeah, they were praying, but praying, but not in a group together. They were praying individually. Plaszow was very terrible. You know, killing were going on there. You see that-- you have that in the-- in the movie. They mention [NON-ENGLISH] you know. I cannot explain to you how-- what that was, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's where they bury all the people. If one was shot, that's where they used to bury them. We used to call it [NON-ENGLISH]. In other words, that was a lot sand and-- and-- and-- and [INAUDIBLE] you know. And [SIGHS] how you call it?

speaker: interviewee
text: They used to just cover it, you know, with-- with some earth. And that's it. That's what-- that's what they used to call it with that name there, you know. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] Killings were going on there every single day. There was no day where people were not killed. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I remember one day, it was Sunday morning around 8 o'clock. And that was, yes, a Sunday. And the [NON-ENGLISH] and other one were drinking all night with women. And in the morning, they got out from the-- from the houses there. And we were working [INAUDIBLE]. And they were passing by next to us. And one of them wanted to go to shot one of-- one-- one of the boys who were working. And one of the women pulled him back, command come-- come back. In other words, she saved-- she was the one who saved that-- that boy just in that time. And that stories were going around every single day.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of things did you do to help get through the time? What were you thinking about?

speaker: interviewee
text: I tell you that-- you do not have time to think. You only were thinking about hunger, how to get a piece of bread. Or you were thinking to get to-- to the evening so to see that piece of bread with that little soup, though it was just plain water, you know, not soup. You only were had in your mind food, not the girls. If you survive or not, you didn't think about it. If you survive, you survive. You don't survive, you don't survive. Didn't matter, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you ever, when you were at Plaszow, were you sick at all? Or did your health ever--

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank God I don't think so I was, no. I may have a cold, you know, but not major-- major, because if I would be sick, I wouldn't be here. They didn't play around with sick people there. Even they had a hospital.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned that when you were in Plaszow you did a lot of different jobs, that you were sent from place to place, detail to detail.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were some of the jobs that you did?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] Well, except when we were-- when they enlarged the camp, we were building a new barracks. Because they send in more people. I used to go out from Plaszow to Krakow. And we used to clean, you know, the offices for-- for the SS. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And then build barracks for the-- for the Ukrainian. Most every day different jobs, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned that there came a time when you were able to-- to get to ask of Schindler.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did that come about? What happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, it was a-- a day like any other day. You got a group of people. You know, I don't know, 12 or 15 people. And they tell us to go to a certain place. And we get into that place, we saw there was a lot of people around there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we hear screaming, and yelling, and shouting, you know. You didn't know what's going on over there. Just about the first thing you think, who knows what they going to do to us, you know? And we hear-- we saw that there was the-- the chief of the Jewish police. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And people there, you see there were a lot people over there in Plaszow. Well, they came after the liquidation of Krakow ghetto. And they still had some money, or jewelry, or something. And they were throwing that at him. And we didn't know why they do that. What's going on over here?

speaker: interviewee
text: And suddenly, his neighbors, by the way, [NON-ENGLISH]. He was the chief of the police. He has got as much power as-- as a SS, you know, over there. And suddenly, he says to the other people that he didn't want to take nothing from them, from the other-- from the other thing. Because he had plenty of money, he didn't-- And he shows his finger at us. And he says-- I'm going to repeat-- say it in Polish. [POLISH] What it means 'let the lowest from lower go.' That's what it means.

speaker: interviewer
text: But we didn't know what that means. So what do you mean, to go where? So after he said that, they took us, and they brought us in a barrack. They closed the door. And if we didn't have to work.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said something's not kosher. I don't like that. So they mention that we're going to Schindler. But I never heard about his name. I didn't know who-- who is Schindler.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I remember he passed by-- by the gate, passed by a friend of mine. Unfortunately, he's not alive anymore. And I asked him-- [? Ignatz ?] was his name. What is Schindler? Who is Schindler?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because you know, I could still sneak out from there. You know, there was a time you could sneak out. So he said, you have nothing left to lose. Go. It cannot be worse than here. Even he didn't know either. But he knew-- he heard about Schindler. But he didn't know what it is then. So that's how I got from Plaszow to Schindler. In the following days, they send us, because Schindler had his factories in Krakow, not in Plaszow. So that-- we were not the only group, 15 people that came, you know, but-- but maybe 200 people. But we were one of the groups. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And what we-- they used to manufacture their pots and pan, you know. But our job was to build ammunition factory because you see, to-- to have a factory in pots and pans, that's not for-- for-- for-- for war, you know. He couldn't-- he couldn't do that. So he started to build a-- a factory from ammunition. And I remember he came in the first day. There was a brick yard, a big place. And so much loaded on it, you know, and we had to clean that. In one day, they have to clean that. And after that one day, I said to myself, I should better stay in Plaszow than to come here. But after that, it started to get better. We started to-- to build that factory. And in between, we used to go to there and make-- help out in pots and pans too, you know. And we used to get the soup, extra soup. In other words, we got a soup at night in the-- in the camp, and then during the lunchtime because a lot of Poles used to work in that factory too. And that's how we built. And I was-- I was tall and skinny, so I was chosen by a-- by a German. You know, by the way, the factory was built by a private company by the name [INAUDIBLE]. That's the biggest German company in Germany now.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I was chosen to work together with a German to put down the windows. You know, they were especially heavy, heavy windows with screws, not-- not [INAUDIBLE]. So I worked with him, and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and we used to work for-- for until we finished that.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was almost finished, the factory. And that was around the end of 1944. But the Russians were closing up. But the factory was never finished.

speaker: interviewer
text: So then we started to take apart the factory. And as we were in the-- in the barracks-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Now, in between, half of the camp was sent out to Mauthausen. Half of the people working with Schindler--

speaker: interviewee
text: Half of the people-- not only from Schindler, there used to be another-- another factory there. But the people used to work there, you know, not only by Schindler, but they used to work in another factory. And they were sent out to Mauthausen. I was the one who was left over there. I stayed there still. And we were at a-- we had a very cramped barrack. You know, there was a lot people. You know, we sleep two in one bed because they didn't have enough room. And when they left, they transported us to this-- to the other barracks. But the people who were sent away, and I think the following night or two nights later, the Germans shot down an Australian plane. And that plane fell on our barrack. And that barrack where we used to stay at two days before, and it caught fire. And 3/4 of the barracks were destroyed. So that time they-- they marching for security reasons. They send us back to Plaszow.

speaker: interviewer
text: So in other words, he stopped working there. Schindler stopped working the pots and pan and everything. That was by, I think, October, 1944. How--

speaker: interviewee
text: And if he can--

speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead. I'm sorry.

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE] and when he came back to Plaszow, that [INAUDIBLE], who was the commander wasn't in there anymore. There was a gentleman, one of the-- one of the people who was in camp too. You know, there was a lot of political prisoners there, German. He became the commander of that camp. And the situation was much better. There was no killing anymore and-- and-- and that at Plaszow. But Schindler kept an eye on us. In other words, he wanted-- he started to build a new factory in Czechoslovakia by the name Brunnlitz. And he wanted his people.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it came around, I think, it must have been the end of '44 or maybe beginning of '45. We were sent to get to Gross Rosen. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] with trains. It took-- I don't know-- about two or three days in trains. But when they stopped on the-- on some stations, so they opened the door, and we saw Schindler walking down on the station to be-- to make sure that every-- that every wagon gets a-- a-- a pail of water.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'll never forget that, you know. He was taking care that we get water. And now, we arrived to Gross Rosen. We stayed there a few days. We didn't work there. We just exercise all day long, from the morning till the evening. Hands up. Hands down and [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: They were ordering you to do exercises, that was--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yes, we-- we-- we didn't work there. You see, there were barracks over there, beautiful barracks. But you're not supposed to touch them. And we used to sleep in a room. But there was maybe 200, 300 people. They used to put the people one with your head this way, and the one with their head down, you know. And that was-- the people who-- who were the-- the leaders of that camp were Poles. They were sent there. Because in-- by the end of 1944, there was-- what you call it-- an uprising in Poland. And all the Poles from the uprising were sent to Gross Rosen. And they became the-- the-- the-- the-- the-- the head of that camp, you know. So I never forget that Pole. He was sleeping on a bed, you know. And you know, when you have 200 people laying in a-- in a room, if you want to go out, so he was so bad. You know, if you make a noise, he came. He start to hit you, you know. But thank God we were there only a few days. And then they put us back on trains, and they sent us to-- to Schindler to Plaszow-- to Brunnlitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Approximately when did you arrive there?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's a good question. It must have been [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] must have been by the-- Gross Rosen-- must have been about the end of '44, I think. Yeah, because I remember when we got to Gross Rosen, it was winter. It was a very cold. So you-- so we still had a few private--

speaker: interviewer
text: We're going to end tape three here. We'll pick this up with tape four.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.
